questions.stem: The fundamental economic problem that gives rise to the study of economics is best described as which of the following?
options: Scarcity: unlimited wants set against limited resources
A shortage of money in the banking system
Greed on the part of consumers
The unfair distribution of income
questions.explanation: Economics begins with scarcity. Human wants are effectively unlimited, but the resources to satisfy them are limited, so choices must be made about how to use those resources.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The opportunity cost of a decision is best defined as which of the following?
options: The tax paid on the chosen option
The sum of all alternatives that were not chosen
The total money spent on the chosen option
The value of the next best alternative that is given up
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the single next best alternative forgone when a choice is made. It is not the money price alone, nor the sum of every rejected option.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A student spends an evening studying instead of working a shift that would have paid 40 cedis. The opportunity cost of studying is closest to which of the following?
options: The cost of the textbooks used
Zero, because studying is free
The 40 cedis of wages forgone
The student's whole term fees
questions.explanation: The next best use of that evening was the paid shift, so the opportunity cost of studying is the 40 cedis of earnings given up. Out of pocket spending on books is a separate, explicit cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The four factors of production are usually listed as land, labour, capital, and which fourth factor?
options: Money
Government
Technology
Enterprise (entrepreneurship)
questions.explanation: The classic four factors are land, labour, capital, and enterprise. Enterprise organises the other three and bears risk. Money is not a factor of production, it is a medium of exchange.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In economics, 'capital' as a factor of production refers chiefly to which of the following?
options: The money a firm holds in the bank
Manufactured goods used to produce other goods, such as machines and tools
The shares traded on the stock exchange
The personal savings of households
questions.explanation: To an economist, capital means produced means of production: machinery, tools, equipment, and buildings used to make other goods. Financial sums and shares are financial capital, not the factor of production itself.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The reward (factor payment) associated with the factor of production labour is which of the following?
options: Rent
Interest
Profit
Wages
questions.explanation: Each factor earns a characteristic payment: land earns rent, labour earns wages, capital earns interest, and enterprise earns profit.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A production possibility frontier (PPF) shows which of the following?
options: The total money supply of an economy
The prices of two goods in a market
The demand for a single good at different prices
The maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce with its resources fully and efficiently used
questions.explanation: The PPF (also called the production possibility curve) traces the maximum output combinations of two goods attainable when all resources are fully and efficiently employed with given technology.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A point lying inside (below) a production possibility frontier represents which situation?
options: Unemployed or underused resources
The maximum possible output
Productive efficiency
An unattainable combination of output
questions.explanation: Points on the frontier are efficient and points beyond it are unattainable with current resources. A point inside the frontier means resources are not fully or efficiently used, for example because of unemployment.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A production possibility frontier is typically drawn bowed outwards (concave to the origin). This shape reflects which idea?
options: Constant opportunity cost
Increasing opportunity cost as more of one good is produced
Zero opportunity cost
Falling opportunity cost as more of one good is produced
questions.explanation: The outward bowed shape shows increasing opportunity cost. As production of one good expands, resources less suited to it must be transferred, so ever larger amounts of the other good are sacrificed.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An outward shift of the entire production possibility frontier most directly represents which of the following?
options: A movement along the frontier
A fall in demand
A rise in the general price level
Economic growth, an increase in productive capacity
questions.explanation: When resources increase or technology improves, the economy can produce more of both goods, so the whole frontier shifts outward. This is the standard depiction of economic growth.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is a positive statement rather than a normative one?
options: A rise in the price of petrol reduces the quantity of petrol demanded
Taxes on the rich are unfair
It would be better if everyone earned the same income
The government ought to spend more on health care
questions.explanation: A positive statement describes what is and can in principle be tested against evidence. The claim about price and quantity is testable. The others express value judgements about what ought to be, which are normative.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A normative economic statement is one that does which of the following?
options: Reports the current rate of inflation
Describes facts that can be tested with data
States a definition agreed by all economists
Involves a value judgement about what ought to be
questions.explanation: Normative statements rest on opinions and value judgements about what should happen. They cannot be settled purely by appeal to facts, unlike positive statements which describe what is.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Microeconomics is best described as the study of which of the following?
options: International exchange rates only
Only the government budget
The economy as a whole, including total output and inflation
Individual markets, firms, and households and their decisions
questions.explanation: Microeconomics looks at the behaviour of individual decision makers such as consumers and firms and at particular markets. Economy wide aggregates such as national output and inflation are the domain of macroeconomics.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Every economy must answer three central questions. Which set correctly lists them?
options: Who to employ, when to retire, and how to save
When to tax, how much to borrow, and where to spend
What to import, how to export, and for whom to trade
What to produce, how to produce it, and for whom to produce
questions.explanation: Because resources are scarce, every society must decide what goods and services to produce, how to produce them (which methods and resources), and for whom they are produced (how output is distributed).
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In a pure market (free enterprise) economy, the three central economic questions are answered chiefly by which mechanism?
options: Central planning by the state
Random allocation
The price mechanism and the decisions of buyers and sellers
International agencies
questions.explanation: In a free market economy, prices act as signals and incentives. The interaction of supply and demand, not a central planner, decides what is produced, how, and for whom.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A mixed economy is best described as one in which which of the following holds?
options: All decisions are made by the state
Both the private market and the government play a role in allocating resources
There is no money, only barter
All decisions are made by private markets alone
questions.explanation: Most real economies are mixed: markets allocate many resources, but the government also intervenes, providing public goods, regulating, and redistributing income. It blends market and command elements.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes a command (planned) economy?
options: Households own all the firms
Prices alone allocate all resources
Resource allocation is decided largely by a central authority rather than by free markets
There is no government involvement
questions.explanation: In a command economy the state owns or directs the major resources and a central plan decides what is produced and how it is shared out, in contrast to the decentralised price mechanism of a market economy.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The assumption that economic agents act 'rationally' usually means which of the following?
options: They always choose the cheapest option
They never make mistakes about the future
They weigh costs and benefits and choose what they expect to be in their best interest
They ignore prices entirely
questions.explanation: Rationality in standard theory means agents compare the expected costs and benefits of their options and pick the one that best serves their objectives, such as maximising utility or profit. It does not require perfect foresight.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A good is described as an economic good (rather than a free good) when which condition holds?
options: It can never be traded
It is supplied by the government
It is always expensive
It is scarce, so that using it has an opportunity cost
questions.explanation: An economic good is scarce relative to wants, so obtaining more of it has an opportunity cost. A free good, such as air in normal conditions, is abundant enough that using it costs nothing in terms of forgone alternatives.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The phrase 'ceteris paribus', used widely in economic analysis, means which of the following?
options: In the long run only
The government decides
Other things being equal (held constant)
Everything changes at once
questions.explanation: Ceteris paribus is Latin for other things being equal. It lets economists isolate the effect of one variable, for example a price change, by assuming all other influences are held constant.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the factor of production 'land'?
options: Mineral deposits and natural farmland
A bank loan
A trained accountant
A delivery van owned by a firm
questions.explanation: In economics, land covers all natural resources: the ground itself, minerals, forests, and water. The van is capital, the loan is finance, and the accountant is labour.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An economy is said to be productively efficient when it operates where on its production possibility frontier?
options: At any point on the frontier itself
At a point well inside the frontier
Only at the midpoint of the frontier
At a point beyond the frontier
questions.explanation: Any point on the frontier is productively efficient: it is impossible to produce more of one good without producing less of the other. Points inside waste resources, and points beyond are unattainable.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When a government chooses to devote more resources to producing weapons rather than food, this trade-off is most directly illustrated by which tool?
options: The balance of payments
The consumer price index
The money supply curve
The production possibility frontier
questions.explanation: The classic guns versus butter choice is shown by moving along a production possibility frontier between two goods. Producing more of one means giving up some of the other, illustrating opportunity cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is the clearest example of an economic 'bad' rather than a good?
options: A loaf of bread
A bottle of clean water
Pollution from a factory
A haircut
questions.explanation: An economic bad is something for which people would pay to have less, because it reduces welfare. Pollution is a classic example. Bread, haircuts, and clean water are goods that raise welfare.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The statement 'unemployment in the country rose to 12 percent last year' is best classified as which of the following?
options: A positive statement
A value judgement
A policy recommendation
A normative statement
questions.explanation: This is a factual claim about what is, and it can be checked against the data. That makes it positive. It contains no judgement about whether the outcome is good or bad.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Specialisation and the division of labour are generally expected to do which of the following?
options: Remove the problem of scarcity entirely
Reduce total output in an economy
Raise productivity and total output
Eliminate the need for any trade
questions.explanation: When workers and firms specialise in tasks they do best, output per worker tends to rise, increasing total production. This greater output must then be exchanged, so specialisation increases the need for trade rather than removing it.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
